topics.title: Solution of Recurrence Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST831 - INTRODUCTION TO ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solution of Recurrence Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — SOLUTION OF RECURRENCE EQUATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **recurrence relation** (or **recurrence equation**) is a mathematical equation that defines a sequence of values $T(n)$ recursively, in terms of one or more of its preceding (typically smaller) values. It expresses the running time $T(n)$ of a recursive algorithm as a function of the input size $n$ and the running time on smaller inputs.

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \Theta(1) & \text{if } n \le c \\ a \cdot T(n/b) + D(n) + C(n) & \text{if } n > c \end{cases}$$

where:
- $a$ = number of sub-problems generated in the recursion,
- $n/b$ = size of each sub-problem (where $b > 1$),
- $D(n)$ = cost of **dividing** the problem,
- $C(n)$ = cost of **conquering** (combining) the sub-problem solutions.

The term $\Theta(1)$ for $n \le c$ is known as the **base condition** or **stopping condition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** A recurrence alone is *incomplete* without an explicit **base case** (e.g., $T(1) = c$ or $T(0) = c$). Examiners specifically award marks for stating the base case clearly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Russian Nesting Doll (Matryoshka)**
> Imagine you have a large wooden doll of size $n$. To "solve" it, you must:
> 1. **Open** it (cost = $D(n)$ — *divide step*),
> 2. Find **$a$** dolls inside, each of size $n/b$ (cost = $T(n/b)$ each — *recursive calls*),
> 3. **Repaint/Glue** them together once solved (cost = $C(n)$ — *combine step*).
>
> You keep opening dolls until you reach the **smallest solid doll** (the **base case** of size 1, which costs a constant). The total time = time spent opening + time spent solving inside dolls + time spent gluing.

**Why do we need recurrences?** Because divide-and-conquer and recursive algorithms are *self-similar* — the time to solve a problem of size $n$ is naturally expressed in terms of the time to solve a smaller problem of the same type.

### 1.3 Why Recurrences are Central in Algorithm Design

- They provide a **precise mathematical model** for divide-and-conquer algorithms.
- Solving them yields the **asymptotic complexity class** ($\Theta$, $O$, or $\Omega$).
- They form the foundation for understanding **Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen's Matrix Multiplication**, and **FFT**.

### 1.4 Categories of Recurrences (Classification)

| Type | Structure | Example |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Homogeneous Linear** | $T(n) = a_1 T(n-1) + a_2 T(n-2) + \dots$ | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) - T(n-2)$ |
| **Non-Homogeneous Linear** | Includes a non-recursive $f(n)$ | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + n$ |
| **Divide-and-Conquer** | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ |
| **Linear Recurrence with Constant Coefficients** | Polynomial in $T$ | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2)$ (Fibonacci) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Methods for Solving Recurrence Equations (KTU-Required)

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus mandates mastery of the following **four primary techniques**:

#### A. Substitution Method (Guessing + Induction)
**Operational Logic:**
1. **Guess** the closed-form bound (e.g., $T(n) = O(n \log n)$).
2. **Substitute** this guess back into the recurrence.
3. **Prove by induction** that the bound holds for all $n \ge n_0$.

**Why it works:** It is the most rigorous, mathematically airtight technique — but it requires the right guess.

**How to guess:** Use the **Recursion Tree** method to draw out the expansion, then identify the dominant level.

#### B. Iteration Method (Backward Substitution / Unrolling)
**Operational Logic:**
1. Repeatedly **expand** (unroll) the recurrence by replacing $T(n/b)$ with its own definition.
2. Continue until a **pattern** is visible.
3. Sum the resulting series and find a closed form.

**Why it works:** Produces a summation expression that can be evaluated directly.

#### C. Recursion Tree Method
**Operational Logic:**
1. Draw a **tree** where each node represents the cost at a recursive level.
2. Compute the **cost per level** = (number of nodes at that level) × (work per node).
3. Sum the costs across all levels + the cost of the leaves.
4. Total = $T(n) = \sum (\text{level costs})$.

**Why it works:** Provides geometric intuition and is excellent for verifying guesses.

#### D. Master Method (Cookbook for Divide-and-Conquer)
**Operational Logic:** Apply three cases based on comparing $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$.

#### E. Change of Variables (Advanced)
Transform a recurrence into a **familiar form** via substitution. E.g., for $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$, let $m = \log n$, yielding $S(m) = S(m/2) + 1$, which is a Master-form recurrence.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **PRINT THIS TABLE** — it is the most heavily-tested reference for ESE questions.

| # | Method | Governing Formula / Condition | Solution Form |
|---|--------|-------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Master Theorem Case 1** | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ |
| 2 | **Master Theorem Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for $k \ge 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$ |
| 3 | **Master Theorem Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ **and** $a \cdot f(n/b) \le c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ (regularity condition) | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ |
| 4 | **Geometric Series** | $\sum_{i=0}^{k} x^i = \dfrac{1 - x^{k+1}}{1 - x}$ | Used in iteration |
| 5 | **Infinite Geometric** | $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} x^i = \dfrac{1}{1-x}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$ | Used for converging trees |
| 6 | **Log Identity** | $\log_b b = 1$, $\quad \log_b a^k = k \log_b a$ | Master method critical step |
| 7 | **Recursion Tree Height** | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) \Rightarrow$ height $= \log_b n$ | Number of levels |
| 8 | **Leaves Cost** | $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$ | Leaf level total work |
| 9 | **Binary Search** | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| 10 | **Merge Sort** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Regularity Condition for Case 3:** The condition $a \cdot f(n/b) \le c \cdot f(n)$ is what separates a *valid* Case 3 application from a *failed* one. KTU examiners frequently test this.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Compiler Design** | Recurrence analysis of recursive-descent parsers |
| **Database Systems** | Cost modeling of B-tree search and merge-join operations |
| **Computer Graphics** | Recurrence cost of recursive ray-tracing & quad-tree subdivision |
| **Networking** | Recursive analysis of hierarchical routing protocols |
| **Machine Learning** | Recurrence cost of recursive neural network & decision tree traversal |
| **Cryptography** | Cost of recursive modular exponentiation (square-and-multiply) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Theorem — Complete Derivation of the Three Cases

The Master Theorem applies to recurrences of the form:
$$T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$$
where $a \ge 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is asymptotically positive.

**Critical Exponent:**
$$n^{\log_b a} = \text{the cost at the leaves}$$

**Intuition:** Compare $f(n)$ (cost at the **root** level) with $n^{\log_b a}$ (cost at the **leaf** level). Whichever dominates dictates the total cost.

#### CASE 1: $f(n)$ grows **slower** than $n^{\log_b a}$ (Leaves Dominate)
$$f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}) \text{ for some } \epsilon > 0$$
$$\Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$$

**Reasoning:** The bulk of the work happens at the leaves, so the total cost scales with the leaf-level cost.

#### CASE 2: $f(n)$ grows at the **same rate** as $n^{\log_b a}$ (Balanced — Every Level Equal)
$$f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n) \text{ for some } k \ge 0$$
$$\Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$$

**Reasoning:** Each of the $\log_b n$ levels contributes equal work, so we multiply the per-level cost by the number of levels.

#### CASE 3: $f(n)$ grows **faster** than $n^{\log_b a}$ (Root Dominates)
$$f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon}) \text{ for some } \epsilon > 0$$
**AND** the regularity condition: $a \cdot f(n/b) \le c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ and large $n$.
$$\Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$$

**Reasoning:** Cost decreases geometrically from root to leaves, so the root dominates the sum.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort)

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**
$a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2: Compute the critical exponent.**
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$$

**Step 3: Compare $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$.**
$$f(n) = n = \Theta(n^1) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^0 n)$$

This matches **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Step 4: Apply Case 2 formula.**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n \cdot \log^1 n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

**Verification via Recursion Tree:**
- Level 0: $n$
- Level 1: $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$
- Level 2: $4 \cdot (n/4) = n$
- $\dots$
- Level $\log_2 n$: $n$
- Total = $n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log n)$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**
$a = 9$, $b = 3$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2: Compute the critical exponent.**
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_3 9} = n^2$$

**Step 3: Compare.**
$$f(n) = n = O(n^{2 - \epsilon}) \text{ for } \epsilon = 1$$

**Step 4: Apply Case 1.**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_3 9}) = \Theta(n^2)$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: $T(n) = T(2n/3) + 1$ (Strassen-like / Linear Search variant)

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**
$a = 1$, $b = 3/2$, $f(n) = 1$.

**Step 2: Compute the critical exponent.**
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_{3/2} 1} = n^0 = 1$$

**Step 3: Compare.**
$$f(n) = 1 = \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^0 n)$$

Matches **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Step 4: Apply Case 2.**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{3/2} 1} \cdot \log^1 n) = \Theta(1 \cdot \log n) = \Theta(\log n)$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4: Iteration Method — $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$

**Step 1: Unroll the recurrence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 3T(n/4) + n^2 \\
&= 3\bigl[3T(n/16) + (n/4)^2\bigr] + n^2 \\
&= 9T(n/16) + 3 \cdot n^2/16 + n^2 \\
&= 9T(n/16) + \frac{3n^2}{16} + n^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Pattern after $i$ unrollings.**
$$T(n) = 3^i T(n/4^i) + n^2 \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} (3/16)^j$$

**Step 3: Stop when $n/4^i = 1$, i.e., $i = \log_4 n$.**
$$T(n) = 3^{\log_4 n} T(1) + n^2 \sum_{j=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} (3/16)^j$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the geometric sum.**
Since $3/16 < 1$, the sum converges:
$$\sum_{j=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} (3/16)^j = \frac{1 - (3/16)^{\log_4 n}}{1 - 3/16} = \Theta(1)$$

**Step 5: Simplify the leaf term.**
$$3^{\log_4 n} = n^{\log_4 3} = O(n^{0.79})$$

**Step 6: Combine.**
$$T(n) = O(n^{0.79}) + n^2 \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^2)$$

This matches **Master Theorem Case 3** since $f(n) = n^2 = \Omega(n^{0.79 + \epsilon})$ ✓

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 5: Substitution Method — $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$

**Guess:** $T(n) = O(n \log n)$, i.e., $T(n) \le c \cdot n \log n$ for some $c > 0$ and $n \ge n_0$.

**Step 1: Substitute the inductive hypothesis.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + n \\
&\le 2 \cdot c \cdot (n/2) \log(n/2) + n \\
&= c \cdot n \log(n/2) + n \\
&= c \cdot n [\log n - \log 2] + n \\
&= c \cdot n \log n - c \cdot n + n \\
&= c \cdot n \log n - (c - 1) n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Complete the induction.**
For $T(n) \le c \cdot n \log n$ to hold, we need:
$$c \cdot n \log n - (c-1) n \le c \cdot n \log n$$

This is satisfied when $(c - 1) n \ge 0$, i.e., $c \ge 1$. ✓

**Base case:** Choose $n_0 = 2$ and a large enough $c$ such that the bound holds. Thus $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ is proved.

---

### 3.7 Worked Example 6: Change of Variables — $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$

**Step 1: Apply the substitution.** Let $n = 2^m$, so $m = \log_2 n$. Define $S(m) = T(2^m)$.

**Step 2: Transform the recurrence.**
$$S(m) = T(2^m) = T(\sqrt{2^m}) + 1 = T(2^{m/2}) + 1 = S(m/2) + 1$$

**Step 3: Solve the transformed recurrence using the Master Method.**
$a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(m) = 1$.
$$m^{\log_2 1} = m^0 = 1$$
Since $f(m) = 1 = \Theta(m^0)$, **Case 2** applies with $k = 0$:
$$S(m) = \Theta(\log m)$$

**Step 4: Back-substitute.**
$$T(n) = S(\log_2 n) = \Theta(\log \log n)$$

---

### 3.8 Analytical Master Method Application Table

| Recurrence | $a$ | $b$ | $n^{\log_b a}$ | $f(n)$ | Comparison | Case | Solution $T(n)$ |
|------------|-----|-----|----------------|--------|------------|------|-----------------|
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | 2 | 2 | $n$ | $n$ | $f(n) = \Theta(n \log^0 n)$ | 2 | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ | 2 | 2 | $n$ | 1 | $f(n) = O(n^{1-\epsilon})$ | 1 | $\Theta(n)$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ | 2 | 2 | $n$ | $n^2$ | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{1+\epsilon})$ | 3 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ | 4 | 2 | $n^2$ | $n$ | $f(n) = O(n^{2-\epsilon})$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ | 4 | 2 | $n^2$ | $n^2$ | $f(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^0 n)$ | 2 | $\Theta(n^2 \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^3$ | 4 | 2 | $n^2$ | $n^3$ | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{2+\epsilon})$ | 3 | $\Theta(n^3)$ |
| $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | 1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | $f(n) = \Theta(1 \cdot \log^0 n)$ | 2 | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 3T(n/3) + n \log n$ | 3 | 3 | $n$ | $n \log n$ | $f(n) = \Omega(n \log^1 n)$ | 3 | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ | 2 | 2 | $n$ | $n \log n$ | $f(n) = \omega(n \log^0 n)$ but no regularity gap | **GAP** | $\Theta(n \log^2 n)$ (Akra–Bazzi) |
| $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + n^2 \log n$ | 8 | 2 | $n^3$ | $n^2 \log n$ | $f(n) = O(n^{3-\epsilon})$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^3)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Method Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Recurrence: T of n equals a T of n over b plus f of n] --> startB{Identify a, b, f of n}
    startB --> startC[Compute n raised to log base b of a]
    startC --> startD{Compare f of n with n raised to log base b of a}
    startD --> startE{f of n is polynomially smaller}
    startE --> startF[Apply Case 1]
    startF --> startG[T of n equals Theta of n raised to log base b of a]
    startD --> startH{f of n is asymptotically equal}
    startH --> startI[Apply Case 2]
    startI --> startJ[T of n equals Theta of n raised to log base b of a times log to the k plus 1 of n]
    startD --> startK{f of n is polynomially larger}
    startK --> startL{Regularity condition a times f of n over b is at most c times f of n for c less than 1}
    startL --> startM[Apply Case 3]
    startM --> startN[T of n equals Theta of f of n]
    startL --> startO[Master Method FAILS: Use Akra-Bazzi or other method]
```

### 4.2 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1["Level 0: n"] --> node1a["Level 1: n/2"]
    root1 --> node1b["Level 1: n/2"]
    node1a --> node2a["Level 2: n/4"]
    node1a --> node2b["Level 2: n/4"]
    node1b --> node2c["Level 2: n/4"]
    node1b --> node2d["Level 2: n/4"]
    node2a --> leaf1["Level 3: n/8"]
    node2a --> leaf2["Level 3: n/8"]
    node2b --> leaf3["Level 3: n/8"]
    node2b --> leaf4["Level 3: n/8"]
    node2c --> leaf5["Level 3: n/8"]
    node2c --> leaf6["Level 3: n/8"]
    node2d --> leaf7["Level 3: n/8"]
    node2d --> leaf8["Level 3: n/8"]
```

**Cost Summary per Level (in the general case):**
- Level 0: $1 \cdot n = n$
- Level 1: $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$
- Level $i$: $2^i \cdot (n/2^i) = n$
- Number of levels = $\log_2 n + 1$
- **Total cost** = $n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log n)$

### 4.3 Method Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[Recurrence Equation] --> startB{Type of Recurrence}
    startB --> startC[Divide and Conquer form aT of n over b plus f of n]
    startB --> startD[Non-standard or non-homogeneous]
    startC --> startE{Master Method Applicable}
    startE --> startF[YES: Use Master Theorem]
    startE --> startG[NO: Use Recursion Tree]
    startD --> startH[Linear Homogeneous]
    startD --> startI[Polynomial or non-polynomial f of n]
    startH --> startJ[Use Characteristic Equation method]
    startI --> startK[Use Change of Variables or Generating Functions]
    startF --> startL[Solved T of n]
    startG --> startL
    startJ --> startL
    startK --> startL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define a **recurrence relation**. Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the **iteration method**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A recurrence relation is an equation that defines a sequence of values recursively in terms of one or more preceding values. It expresses the running time of a recursive algorithm as a function of the input size.

**Solving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ by iteration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + 1 \\
&= 2\bigl[2T(n/4) + 1\bigr] + 1 = 4T(n/4) + 2 + 1 \\
&= 4\bigl[2T(n/8) + 1\bigr] + 3 = 8T(n/8) + 4 + 3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= 2^k T(n/2^k) + (2^k - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Stopping at $k = \log_2 n$ (when $n/2^k = 1$):
$$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} T(1) + (2^{\log_2 n} - 1) = n \cdot 1 + (n - 1) = 2n - 1 = \Theta(n) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

What is the **Master Theorem**? State the three cases with their conditions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Master Theorem provides a direct cookbook method to solve recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, where $a \ge 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is asymptotically positive. Let $n^{\log_b a}$ be the critical exponent.

**Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.

**Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \ge 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$.

**Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ **and** the regularity condition $a f(n/b) \le c f(n)$ holds for some $c < 1$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n \log n$ using the **Master Theorem** (or state why it is inapplicable). Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1/T(n-1)$ approximately for $T(1) = 1$ using the **guess and verify (substitution) method** OR **iteration method**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a): $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n \log n$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
$$a = 3, \quad b = 4, \quad f(n) = n \log n$$

**Step 2: Compute critical exponent.**
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$$

**Step 3: Compare $f(n) = n \log n$ with $n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$.**
Since $n \log n = \omega(n^{0.792})$ and in fact $n \log n = \Omega(n^{0.792 + \epsilon})$ for any $\epsilon < 1$, we attempt **Case 3**.

**Step 4: Verify the regularity condition.**
We need $a \cdot f(n/b) \le c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$:

$$
3 \cdot f(n/4) = 3 \cdot (n/4) \log(n/4) = \frac{3n}{4} (\log n - 2) \le c \cdot n \log n
$$

For large $n$, $\frac{3n}{4} \log n \le c \cdot n \log n$ holds with $c = 3/4 < 1$. ✓

**Step 5: Apply Case 3.**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

---

#### Part (b): $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1/T(n-1)$, with $T(1) = 1$ (7 Marks)

**Approach: Iteration + Guess.**

**Step 1: Unroll the recurrence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + \frac{1}{T(n-1)} \\
T(n-1) &= T(n-2) + \frac{1}{T(n-2)} \\
T(n-2) &= T(n-3) + \frac{1}{T(n-3)} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
T(2) &= T(1) + \frac{1}{T(1)} = 1 + 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Build the summation.**
We sum the sequence $T(2), T(3), \dots, T(n)$:
$$T(n)^2 - T(1)^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \bigl[T(k+1)^2 - T(k)^2\bigr]$$

Using the identity $(a+b)^2 - a^2 = 2ab + b^2$ with $a = T(k)$, $b = 1/T(k)$:
$$T(k+1)^2 - T(k)^2 = 2 \cdot T(k) \cdot \frac{1}{T(k)} + \frac{1}{T(k)^2} = 2 + \frac{1}{T(k)^2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute back into the recurrence and bound.**

Since $T(k) \ge 1$ for all $k \ge 1$, the term $1/T(k)^2 \le 1$. Therefore:
$$T(n)^2 - 1 = 2(n-1) + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{1}{T(k)^2} \le 2(n-1) + (n-1) = 3(n-1)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $T(n)$.**
$$T(n)^2 \le 1 + 3(n-1) = 3n - 2 \le 3n$$
$$T(n) \le \sqrt{3n} = \Theta(\sqrt{n})$$

**Step 5: Verify with lower bound.**
Since $1/T(k)^2 > 0$, we have $T(n)^2 \ge 2n - 1$, so $T(n) \ge \sqrt{2n - 1} = \Omega(\sqrt{n})$.

**Final answer:**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(\sqrt{n})}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ using **Master Theorem** OR state that it falls in the **gap** and solve it using the **Akra–Bazzi method** or **Recursion Tree**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/4) + n$ using the **Recursion Tree method**. Verify with substitution. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a): $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Apply Master Theorem.**
$$a = 2, \quad b = 2, \quad n^{\log_b a} = n, \quad f(n) = n \log n$$

**Step 2: Check each case.**
- **Case 1:** Requires $f(n) = O(n^{1-\epsilon})$. But $n \log n = \omega(n)$. ✗
- **Case 2:** Requires $f(n) = \Theta(n \log^k n)$. Here $f(n) = n \log n$, so $k = 1$, and $n \log n = \Theta(n \log^1 n)$. But the condition is $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ — for Case 2 we need $f(n) \le c \cdot n^{\log_b a} \log^k n$ for the upper bound and $f(n) \ge d \cdot n^{\log_b a} \log^k n$ for the lower bound, which **fails** because $n \log n$ grows faster than $n$ by more than a polylog factor.
- **Case 3:** Requires $f(n) = \Omega(n^{1+\epsilon})$. Here $n \log n$ is not polynomially (i.e., $n^\epsilon$) larger than $n$. ✗

**Conclusion:** **Master Theorem does not apply** (gap between cases).

**Step 3: Use the Recursion Tree method.**

- Level 0: $n \log n$
- Level 1: $2 \cdot (n/2) \log(n/2) = n (\log n - 1)$
- Level 2: $4 \cdot (n/4) \log(n/4) = n (\log n - 2)$
- Level $i$: $n (\log n - i)$
- Number of levels: $\log_2 n$ (until $n/2^i = 1$)

**Step 4: Sum the geometric-arithmetic hybrid series.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n(\log n - i) + n^{\log_2 2} \cdot T(1) \\
&= n \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} (\log n - i) + n \\
&= n \left[ \log_2 n \cdot \log_2 n - \frac{(\log_2 n - 1)\log_2 n}{2} \right] + n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify.**
The dominant term is $n \cdot (\log_2 n)^2$ minus lower-order terms, so:
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)}$$

---

#### Part (b): $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/4) + n$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Draw the recursion tree.**
- **Root:** cost $= n$
- **Level 1:** two subproblems of sizes $n/2$ and $n/4$; costs = $n/2$ and $n/4$; total = $3n/4$
- **Level 2:** four subproblems of sizes $n/4, n/8, n/8, n/16$; costs = $n/4 + n/8 + n/8 + n/16 = 9n/16$
- **Level $i$:** total cost = $(3/4)^i \cdot n$

**Step 2: Identify the geometric ratio.**
Each level multiplies the previous cost by $3/4$. Since $3/4 < 1$, the series converges.

**Step 3: Sum the series.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= n + \frac{3n}{4} + \frac{9n}{16} + \frac{27n}{64} + \cdots \\
&= n \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^i \\
&= n \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 3/4} = 4n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify with the substitution method.**
**Guess:** $T(n) \le c \cdot n$ for some $c > 0$ and $n \ge n_0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + T(n/4) + n \\
&\le c \cdot (n/2) + c \cdot (n/4) + n \\
&= c n \left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4}\right) + n \\
&= \frac{3c n}{4} + n
\end{aligned}
$$

We need $\frac{3c n}{4} + n \le c n$, i.e., $n \le \frac{c n}{4}$, i.e., $c \ge 4$. ✓

**Final answer:**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the base case** (e.g., $T(1) = c$). Examiners allocate **1 mark** explicitly for this.
> 2. **Mismatching log bases** when computing $n^{\log_b a}$. Always rewrite $\log_b a$ explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping the regularity condition check** in Case 3. Even if the polynomial condition holds, **you must verify** $a f(n/b) \le c f(n)$.
> 4. **Failing to show unrolling steps** in iteration. Examiners want to see at least 3 unrollings to confirm the pattern.
> 5. **Stating the answer as $O(\cdot)$ instead of $\Theta(\cdot)$** when Master Theorem is used. Master Theorem yields a **tight** bound — use $\Theta$.
> 6. **Assuming $a, b$ are integers.** They may be non-integer constants (e.g., $b = 3/2$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before Exam)**

- [ ] **Master Theorem applies ONLY** to recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \ge 1, b > 1$.
- [ ] The **critical exponent** is $n^{\log_b a}$ — it represents the **leaf-level cost** of the recursion tree.
- [ ] **Case 1** = Leaves dominate = $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- [ ] **Case 2** = Balanced (every level equal) = $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
- [ ] **Case 3** = Root dominates = $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$, **provided regularity condition holds**.
- [ ] **Master Theorem has a gap** when $f(n)$ is larger than $n^{\log_b a}$ by less than a polynomial factor (e.g., $f(n) = n \log n$ when $n^{\log_b a} = n$).
- [ ] **Iteration method** requires careful handling of **geometric series** — remember $\sum_{i=0}^{k} r^i = \frac{1 - r^{k+1}}{1 - r}$.
- [ ] **Recursion Tree method** = visualize + sum level costs.
- [ ] **Substitution method** = guess + substitute + inductive proof.
- [ ] **Change of variables** is used for non-standard recurrences (e.g., $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + \dots$).
- [ ] **Five standard recurrences** to memorize: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ → $\Theta(n \log n)$, $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ → $\Theta(n)$, $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ → $\Theta(\log n)$, $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ → $\Theta(n^2)$, $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ → $\Theta(n^2)$.
- [ ] Always write $T(n) = \Theta(\cdot)$ — not $O(\cdot)$ — when the answer is provably tight.
- [ ] The **base case** must accompany every recurrence; without it, the recurrence is mathematically incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iteration Method and Recursion Tree Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST831 - INTRODUCTION TO ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Iteration Method and Recursion Tree Methods # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Recurrence Relations — The Foundation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > A **recurrence relation** is an equation (or inequality) that describes a function in terms of **its own value on smaller inputs**. In the context of algorithm analysis, recurrences arise naturally when an algorithm recursively breaks a problem of size $n$ into sub-problems of smaller size, and the running time on the input of size $n$ is expressed as a function of the running times on the sub-problems. Mathematically, a recurrence has the form: $$T(n) = \begin{cases} \Theta(1) & \text{if } n \leq n_0 \\ a \cdot T(n/b) + f(n) & \text{if } n > n_0 \end{cases}$$ Where: - $T(n)$ = Time complexity on input of size $n$ - $a$ = Number of sub-problems in the recursion - $n/b$ = Size of each sub-problem (assumes equal division) - $f(n)$ = Cost of work done at the **root level** (dividing + combining) - $n_0$ = Base case threshold (often $T(1) = \Theta(1)$) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Russian Nesting Doll" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** > Imagine a **Matryoshka Doll** (Russian nesting doll). You open the outer doll ($n$), and inside you find $a$ smaller dolls of size $n/b$. Open one of those, and you find $a$ even smaller dolls, and so on, until you reach the tiniest solid doll (the **base case**). > > The **total work** of the algorithm is the work done at *every* level of this doll hierarchy. To find the total, you must: > 1. Count how many dolls (sub-problems) exist at each level. > 2. Measure how much work each doll does (cost per sub-problem). > 3. Sum up the work across *all* levels. > > This is exactly what the **Iteration Method** and the **Recursion Tree Method** do! ## 1.3 Three Standard Methods to Solve Recurrences | Method | Core Idea | Best For | |---|---|---| | **Substitution Method** | Guess the answer, then prove by induction | Verifying bounds | | **Iteration (Unrolling) Method** | Repeatedly expand $T(n)$ until a pattern emerges | Simple linear recurrences | | **Recursion Tree Method** | Draw a tree of recursive calls, sum costs per level | Divide-and-conquer recurrences | | **Master Theorem** | Plug into one of 3 cases | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ form | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** > This topic falls under **Module 1: Introduction to Algorithm Analysis**, sub-topic "Asymptotic Notations and Solving Recurrences". Students must master the Iteration and Recursion Tree methods **by hand** before using the Master Theorem (which is a shortcut over both). ## 1.4 The Two Methods We'll Master Today - **Iteration Method**: Algebraic unrolling — substitute $T(n)$ into itself repeatedly until $T(1)$ appears, then sum the geometric/algebraic series. - **Recursion Tree Method**: Visual unrolling — draw the recursion as a tree, compute total cost per level, then sum all levels. Both methods yield the **same answer**; the recursion tree simply makes the level-by-level work visible, which is excellent for board exams. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Iteration Method — Step-by-Step Philosophy The Iteration Method (also called the **Unrolling Method** or **Repeated Substitution Method**) is the most mechanical approach to solving recurrences. It does not require clever guessing. ### Operational Steps: 1. **Write the recurrence** in standard form: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$. 2. **Substitute** $T(n/b)$ by replacing $n$ with $n/b$ in the recurrence. 3. **Repeat substitution** $k$ times until the term $T(n/b^k)$ becomes $T(1)$ (the base case). 4. **Stop condition**: Set $n/b^k = 1 \implies b^k = n \implies k = \log_b n$. 5. **Sum the unrolled terms** to get $T(n)$ as a closed-form expression. ### The Unrolled Form (General Template): For $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$: $$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$ $$= a \left[ a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^2}\right) + f\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) \right] + f(n)$$ $$= a^2 T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^2}\right) + a \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$ After $k$ substitutions: $$T(n) = a^k T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^k}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a^i f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right)$$ When $k = \log_b n$, the term $a^k T(1) = a^{\log_b n} \cdot \Theta(1) = n^{\log_b a} \cdot \Theta(1)$. ## 2.2 The Recursion Tree Method — Visual Decomposition The Recursion Tree Method **draws** the recursion as a tree, where: - **Each node** represents the cost of a single sub-problem (excluding the cost of its children). - **Each level** of the tree contains sub-problems of equal size. - **Leaves** correspond to the base case $T(1)$. ### Construction Rules: 1. **Root node** holds the value $f(n)$. 2. **Each node with sub-problem size $m$** has $a$ children, each holding a sub-problem of size $m/b$, and each child has its own $f(m/b)$. 3. **Total work at level $i$** = (number of nodes at level $i$) $\times$ (work per node at level $i$) = $a^i \cdot f(n/b^i)$. 4. **Tree height** = $h = \log_b n$ (until sub-problem size reaches 1). 5. **Number of leaves** = $a^h = a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$. 6. **Total cost** = (Sum of work at all internal levels) + (Work at leaves). $$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} a^i \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right) + a^h \cdot T(1)$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS** | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Tree height | $h = \log_b n$ | Levels until leaves | | 2 | Nodes at level $i$ | $a^i$ | Sub-problem count | | 3 | Size of sub-problem at level $i$ | $n / b^i$ | Argument to $f$ | | 4 | Work at level $i$ | $a^i \cdot f(n / b^i)$ | Per-level cost | | 5 | Number of leaves | $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$ | Base-case calls | | 6 | Leaf-level total work | $\Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | Recursion-driven cost | | 7 | General sum | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{h-1} a^i f(n/b^i)$ | Internal-level sum | ### Common Series You'll Encounter (Use in Summation) | Series | Closed Form | Condition | |---|---|---| | $\sum_{i=0}^{k} x^i$ | $\frac{x^{k+1} - 1}{x - 1}$ | $x \neq 1$ | | $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} x^i$ | $\frac{1}{1-x}$ | $\vert x \vert < 1$ | | $\sum_{i=0}^{k} i \cdot x^i$ | $\frac{x - (k+1)x^{k+1} + kx^{k+2}}{(1-x)^2}$ | $x \neq 1$ | | $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_b n - 1} b^i$ | $\Theta(n) - 1$ | geometric, ratio $b$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **PITFALL:** When using geometric series, **always** identify the common ratio first, then apply the formula. KTU examiners frequently test students on this! ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Merge Sort / Quick Sort Analysis**: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ → $\Theta(n \log n)$ — used in databases, signal processing. - **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication**: $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ → $\Theta(n^{2.807...})$ — used in graphics and ML. - **Binary Search**: $T(n) = T(n/2) + \Theta(1)$ → $\Theta(\log n)$ — used in IP routing, dictionaries. - **FFT (Cooley-Tukey)**: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ → $\Theta(n \log n)$ — used in audio/image compression. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXHAUSTIVE WORKED EXAMPLES — KTU Board Exam Style** > Each example shows *every* algebraic step. Do not skip ahead. --- ## Example 1: $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + \Theta(n^2)$ — Using Iteration Method ### Step 1: Write the recurrence explicitly $$T(n) = 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + cn^2$$ where $c > 0$ is a constant, and $T(1) = \Theta(1)$. ### Step 2: First Unrolling (substitute $T(n/4)$) $$T(n) = 3 \left[ 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^2}\right) + c\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right)^2 \right] + cn^2$$ $$T(n) = 3^2 T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^2}\right) + 3c \cdot \frac{n^2}{4^2} + cn^2$$ ### Step 3: Second Unrolling (substitute $T(n/4^2)$) $$T(n) = 3^2 \left[ 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^3}\right) + c\!\left(\frac{n}{4^2}\right)^2 \right] + 3c \cdot \frac{n^2}{4^2} + cn^2$$ $$T(n) = 3^3 T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^3}\right) + 3^2 c \cdot \frac{n^2}{4^4} + 3c \cdot \frac{n^2}{4^2} + cn^2$$ ### Step 4: Pattern Recognition (after $k$ unrollings) $$T(n) = 3^k T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^k}\right) + cn^2 \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 3^i \cdot \frac{1}{4^{2i}}$$ $$T(n) = 3^k T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^k}\right) + cn^2 \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 3^i \cdot 4^{-2i}$$ $$T(n) = 3^k T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^k}\right) + cn^2 \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i$$ ### Step 5: Stop the unrolling at the base case Set $\frac{n}{4^k} = 1 \implies 4^k = n \implies k = \log_4 n$. ### Step 6: Evaluate the leaf term $$3^k T(1) = 3^{\log_4 n} \cdot \Theta(1) = n^{\log_4 3} \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^{\log_4 3})$$ Since $\log_4 3 \approx 0.792 < 2$, this term is **polynomially smaller** than $n^2$. ### Step 7: Evaluate the geometric sum The ratio is $r = 3/16 < 1$, so the infinite geometric series converges: $$\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i < \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i = \frac{1}{1 - 3/16} = \frac{16}{13} = \Theta(1)$$ ### Step 8: Combine the results $$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_4 3}) + cn^2 \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^2)$$ ### ✅ Final Answer: $$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)}$$ --- ## Example 2: $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$ — Using Recursion Tree Method > [!NOTE] > This is the **classic "unbalanced" recursion** (e.g., the worst case of QuickSort's partition step). The sub-problems are **not equal size**, so the recursion tree is **not perfectly balanced**. ### Step 1: Build the recursion tree - **Root (level 0):** one sub-problem of size $n$, cost $cn$. - **Level 1:** two sub-problems of sizes $n/3$ and $2n/3$, costs $c(n/3)$ and $c(2n/3)$. Total level cost = $c(n/3) + c(2n/3) = cn$. - **Level 2:** four sub-problems of sizes $n/9, 2n/9, 2n/9, 4n/9$. Total cost = $cn$. **Observation:** The total cost at **every level** is exactly $cn$! ### Step 2: Determine the longest path (tree height) The longest path is the one that keeps splitting off the larger piece ($2n/3$): $$n \to \frac{2n}{3} \to \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 n \to \cdots \to 1$$ Solving $\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^h n = 1$: $$h = \log_{3/2} n = \frac{\log n}{\log(3/2)}$$ ### Step 3: Sum costs across all levels Number of levels $= h = \log_{3/2} n$. Cost per level $= cn$. $$T(n) = cn \cdot h = cn \cdot \log_{3/2} n = \Theta(n \log n)$$ ### ✅ Final Answer: $$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$ --- ## Example 3: $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ — Using Recursion Tree Method ### Step 1: Root level work - **Level 0:** 1 node, work per node $= n$, total $= n$. ### Step 2: Subsequent levels - **Level 1:** $a^1 = 4$ nodes, each of size $n/2$. Work per node $= n/2$. Total $= 4 \cdot (n/2) = 2n$. - **Level 2:** $a^2 = 16$ nodes, each of size $n/4$. Work per node $= n/4$. Total $= 16 \cdot (n/4) = 4n$. - **Level $i$:** $4^i$ nodes, each of size $n/2^i$. Work per node $= n/2^i$. Total $= 4^i \cdot n/2^i = n \cdot (4/2)^i = n \cdot 2^i$. ### Step 3: Determine the height and leaf cost Height $h = \log_2 n$ (when $n/2^h = 1$). Leaf-level total work: $4^h \cdot T(1) = 4^{\log_2 n} = n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$. ### Step 4: Sum across all levels $$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} n \cdot 2^i + n^2$$ The sum is a **growing geometric series** (ratio 2): $$\sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n \cdot 2^i = n \cdot (2^{\log_2 n} - 1) = n(n - 1) = \Theta(n^2)$$ ### Step 5: Combine $$T(n) = \Theta(n^2) + n^2 = \Theta(n^2)$$ ### ✅ Final Answer: $$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notice the leaf term dominated** because the work at leaves ($n^2$) grows much faster than work at any internal level. The recursion tree makes this dominance *visible* — this is why the method is so powerful for board exams. --- ## Example 4: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ — Using Iteration Method ### Step 1: Unroll $$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1$$ $$= 2 \left[ 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^2}\right) + 1 \right] + 1 = 2^2 T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^2}\right) + 2 + 1$$ $$= 2^2 \left[ 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^3}\right) + 1 \right] + 3 = 2^3 T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^3}\right) + 4 + 2 + 1$$ ### Step 2: Pattern after $k$ unrollings $$T(n) = 2^k T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^i = 2^k T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + (2^k - 1)$$ ### Step 3: Stop at base case Set $n/2^k = 1 \implies k = \log_2 n$. ### Step 4: Substitute $$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot \Theta(1) + 2^{\log_2 n} - 1 = n + n - 1 = 2n - 1$$ ### ✅ Final Answer: $$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n)}$$ --- ## Analytical Matrix — Recurrence-to-Result Lookup | Recurrence | Method | Tree Pattern | Final Bound | |---|---|---|---| | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | Iteration | Decreasing ($1, 1, 1, \ldots$) | $\Theta(\log n)$ | | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ | Iteration | Growing geometric | $\Theta(n)$ | | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | Recursion tree | **Balanced** ($n, n, n, \ldots$) | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | | $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ | Recursion tree | Growing ($n, 2n, 4n, \ldots$) | $\Theta(n^2)$ | | $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ | Iteration | Shrinking | $\Theta(n^2)$ | | $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ | Recursion tree | Constant per level | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Comparison Flow — Choosing a Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Given Recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)"] --> B{"Question Type?"} B -- "Need to Verify a Guess" --> C["Substitution Method
(Guess and Induct)"] B -- "Mechanical / Direct Solve" --> D{"Sub-problem Sizes Equal?"} D -- "Yes" --> E["Iteration Method
(Algebraic Unrolling)"] D -- "No or Complex" --> F["Recursion Tree Method
(Visual Level-by-Level Sum)"] E --> G["Apply Geometric/
Algebraic Sum"] F --> G G --> H["Closed-Form Asymptotic Bound
(Theta, Big-O, Omega)"] H --> I{"Match Master
Theorem Form?"} I -- "Yes" --> J["Cross-Check with
Master Theorem"] I -- "No" --> K["Final Answer:
Iteration or Tree Result"] ``` ## 4.2 Recursion Tree — Block-Level Functional Architecture for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort) ```mermaid flowchart TD L0["Level 0: Root
1 sub-problem of size n
Work per node = n
Level Total = 1n = n"] L0 --> L1A["Level 1: Left Child
Size n/2, Work = n/2"] L0 --> L1B["Level 1: Right Child
Size n/2, Work = n/2"] L1A --> L2A["Level 2: Size n/4
Work = n/4"] L1A --> L2B["Level 2: Size n/4
Work = n/4"] L1B --> L2C["Level 2: Size n/4
Work = n/4"] L1B --> L2D["Level 2: Size n/4
Work = n/4"] L2A --> L3A["Leaf: T(1) = Theta(1)"] L2A --> L3B["Leaf: T(1) = Theta(1)"] L2B --> L3C["Leaf: T(1) = Theta(1)"] L2B --> L3D["Leaf: T(1) = Theta(1)"] L2C --> L3E["Leaf: T(1) = Theta(1)"] L2C --> L3F["Leaf: T(1) = Theta(1)"] L2D --> L3G["Leaf: T(1) = Theta(1)"] L2D --> L3H["Leaf: T(1) = Theta(1)"] ``` ### Architecture Annotation: - **Total Levels:** $\log_2 n + 1$ (including leaves). - **Work per Internal Level:** $n$ (constant). - **Total Internal Work:** $n \cdot \log_2 n$. - **Leaf Work:** $n \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n)$. - **Grand Total:** $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$. ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Identifying Recurrence Pattern ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Examine f(n)"] --> B{"Compare f(n) vs
n^(log_b a)"} B -- "f(n) grows slower
(polynomially smaller)" --> C["Leaf-Dominant Case
Leaves contribute most
Answer: Theta of n^(log_b a)"] B -- "f(n) grows at
same rate" --> D["Balanced Case
All levels contribute
Answer: Theta of f(n) * log n"] B -- "f(n) grows faster
(polynomially larger)" --> E["Root-Dominant Case
Root contributes most
Answer: Theta of f(n)"] C --> F["Example: T(n)=4T(n/2)+n
= Theta of n squared"] D --> G["Example: T(n)=2T(n/2)+n
= Theta of n log n"] E --> H["Example: T(n)=2T(n/2)+n squared
= Theta of n squared"] ``` ## 4.4 Process Block Diagram — The Iteration Method Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Identify a, b, f(n)
from T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)"] --> S2["Step 2: Perform First Unroll
T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Perform Second Unroll
T(n) = a squared T(n/b squared) + a f(n/b) + f(n)"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Detect Pattern after k unrollings
T(n) = a^k T(n/b^k) + sum over i of a^i f(n/b^i)"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Set n/b^k = 1
Solve for k = log_b n"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Substitute k back
T(n) = a^(log_b n) T(1) + sum"] S6 --> S7["Step 7: Simplify Leaf Term
a^(log_b n) = n^(log_b a)"] S7 --> S8["Step 8: Evaluate the Sum
Use geometric series or other identities"] S8 --> S9["Step 9: Combine and Take Asymptotic Bound
Final Answer: Theta expression"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **What is a recurrence relation? Give an example relevant to algorithm analysis.** **Model Answer:** A recurrence relation is an equation that defines a function $T(n)$ in terms of its own values on smaller inputs. It is the standard way to express the running time of recursive algorithms. **Example:** The recurrence for **Merge Sort** is: $$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn, \quad T(1) = O(1)$$ Here, the problem of size $n$ is divided into 2 sub-problems of size $n/2$, with linear cost $cn$ for the merging step. **[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1 mark example + 1 mark explanation of terms]** --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between the Iteration Method and the Recursion Tree Method for solving recurrences.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Iteration Method | Recursion Tree Method | |---|---|---| | **Approach** | Purely algebraic — substitute repeatedly | Visual — draw a tree of sub-problems | | **Visualization** | None (textual algebra) | Full tree diagram | | **Best For** | Simple, equal-size sub-problems | Divide-and-conquer with imbalanced splits | | **Output** | A summation expression | Per-level costs summed explicitly | | **Complexity** | Easy to write but error-prone in algebra | Easier to spot dominant level visually | **[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark each for approach + best use case + 1 mark for the comparison table]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]** **Solve the following recurrence using the Recursion Tree Method:** $$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2, \quad T(1) = 1$$ #### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Construct the recursion tree and compute the cost at each level. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify the components** - $a = 2$ (number of sub-problems) - $b = 2$ (division factor) - $f(n) = n^2$ (root cost) **Step 2: Build the tree level by level** | Level $i$ | Number of Nodes | Sub-problem Size | Work per Node | Total Level Work | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | $2^0 = 1$ | $n$ | $n^2$ | $n^2$ | | 1 | $2^1 = 2$ | $n/2$ | $(n/2)^2 = n^2/4$ | $2 \cdot n^2/4 = n^2/2$ | | 2 | $2^2 = 4$ | $n/4$ | $n^2/16$ | $4 \cdot n^2/16 = n^2/4$ | | 3 | $2^3 = 8$ | $n/8$ | $n^2/64$ | $8 \cdot n^2/64 = n^2/8$ | | $i$ | $2^i$ | $n/2^i$ | $n^2/4^i$ | $2^i \cdot n^2/4^i = n^2/2^i$ | **General pattern:** Total work at level $i$ is $n^2/2^i$. **Step 3: Determine the tree height** The recursion stops when $n/2^h = 1$, i.e., $h = \log_2 n$. **Step 4: Compute the leaf-level work** Number of leaves $= 2^h = 2^{\log_2 n} = n$. Work at each leaf $= T(1) = 1$. Total leaf work $= n$. #### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Sum the costs and derive the closed-form solution. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Sum internal levels (geometric series with ratio $1/2$)** $$\sum_{i=0}^{h-1} \frac{n^2}{2^i} = n^2 \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^i$$ This is a geometric series with first term 1, ratio $1/2$: $$n^2 \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^{\log_2 n}}{1 - 1/2} = n^2 \cdot 2 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right) = 2n^2 - 2n$$ **Step 2: Add leaf-level work** $$T(n) = 2n^2 - 2n + n = 2n^2 - n$$ **Step 3: Apply asymptotic notation** $$T(n) = 2n^2 - n = \Theta(n^2)$$ ### ✅ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)}$ **[Marks distribution: 2 marks for tree construction + 2 marks for level work formula + 3 marks for sum and asymptotic]** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **Solve the following recurrence using the Iteration Method:** $$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n - 1, \quad T(1) = 0$$ #### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Perform the iteration/unrolling and obtain a general expression for $T(n)$ after $k$ substitutions. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: First unrolling** $$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n - 1$$ **Step 2: Second unrolling** $$T(n) = 2\left[ 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n}{2} - 1 \right] + n - 1$$ $$T(n) = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + n - 2 + n - 1 = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2n - 3$$ **Step 3: Third unrolling** $$T(n) = 4\left[ 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n}{4} - 1 \right] + 2n - 3$$ $$T(n) = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + n - 4 + 2n - 3 = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3n - 7$$ **Step 4: Pattern recognition** After $k$ unrollings: $$T(n) = 2^k T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + kn - (2^k - 1)$$ #### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Use the base case condition and derive the closed-form asymptotic bound. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Apply the base case condition** Set $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, giving $k = \log_2 n$. **Step 2: Substitute $k$ into the general expression** $$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} T(1) + n \log_2 n - (2^{\log_2 n} - 1)$$ $$T(n) = n \cdot 0 + n \log_2 n - (n - 1) = n \log_2 n - n + 1$$ **Step 3: Apply asymptotic notation** $$T(n) = n \log_2 n - n + 1 = \Theta(n \log n)$$ ### ✅ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$ **[Marks distribution: 3 marks for unrolling steps + 2 marks for pattern recognition + 2 marks for asymptotic bound]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the base case in iteration:** Many students unroll to $T(n) = a^k T(n/b^k) + \ldots$ but forget to substitute $T(1)$. You **must** convert the leaf term, or you will lose 2-3 marks. > > 2. **Misidentifying the ratio in geometric series:** When summing $a^i f(n/b^i)$, students often mistake the common ratio. **Check carefully**: it is $a \cdot f(\text{ratio})/f(\text{base})$, or directly compute the ratio between consecutive level costs from the tree. > > 3. **Confusing the height for unbalanced recursions:** For $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + f(n)$, the height is $\log_{3/2} n$, **not** $\log_3 n$ or $\log_2 n$. Always use the **slowest-shrinking branch**. > > 4. **Skipping the asymptotic step:** Writing $T(n) = 2n^2 - n$ is **not** the same as $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$. The latter is what KTU expects. Always close with the $\Theta / O / \Omega$ bound. > > 5. **Forgetting the leaf-level cost in the recursion tree:** Many students sum only internal levels and forget that leaves contribute $a^h \cdot T(1)$. Add this term! --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Iteration & Recursion Tree Methods** ### 🔁 Iteration Method - ✅ Use when sub-problems are **equal-sized** (e.g., $n/2, n/2$). - ✅ Substitute $T(n)$ recursively until $T(1)$ appears. - ✅ General formula: $T(n) = a^k T(n/b^k) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a^i f(n/b^i)$. - ✅ Stop condition: $k = \log_b n$. - ✅ Leaf term: $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$. - ✅ Always apply a **geometric/arithmetic series formula** at the end. - ✅ Conclude with an **asymptotic bound** ($\Theta$). ### 🌳 Recursion Tree Method - ✅ Use when you want a **visual breakdown** of work per level. - ✅ **Root level** work = $f(n)$. - ✅ Work at level $i$ = $a^i \cdot f(n/b^i)$. - ✅ Tree height $h = \log_b n$ (equal splits) or $\log_{b/\alpha} n$ (unbalanced, where $\alpha < 1$ is the largest sub-problem fraction). - ✅ Number of leaves = $n^{\log_b a}$, each contributing $T(1)$. - ✅ Sum: $T(n) = (\text{internal sum}) + (\text{leaf sum})$. - ✅ Identify the **dominant level** (root, leaves, or balanced) — this often reveals the answer. ### 🎯 Cross-Cutting Tips - ✅ If $f(n)$ is **polynomially smaller** than $n^{\log_b a}$: leaves dominate → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$. - ✅ If $f(n)$ is **polynomially larger** than $n^{\log_b a}$: root dominates → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$. - ✅ If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \geq 0$: balanced → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$. - ✅ Always **check the base case** $T(1)$ explicitly in the final substitution. - ✅ KTU board exam preference: **draw the recursion tree** even when solving via iteration — it earns easy method marks. ### 📚 Key Recurrences to Memorize | Recurrence | Solution | Algorithm | |---|---|---| | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Binary Search | | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Tree traversal (sum of leaves) | | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Merge Sort | | $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Naive matrix operations | | $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Worst-case Quicksort | | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$ | Strassen's algorithm |

topics.title: Trees - Binary Trees: level and height of the tree, complete-binary tree representation using array, tree traversals (Recursive and non-recursive), applications. Binary Search Tree: creation, insertion, deletion, and search operations, applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST831 - INTRODUCTION TO ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trees - Binary Trees: level and height of the tree, complete-binary tree representation using array, tree traversals (Recursive and non-recursive), applications. Binary Search Tree: creation, insertion, deletion, and search operations, applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Binary Tree — Formal KTU Definition

A **Binary Tree (BT)** is a hierarchical, non-linear data structure in which each node has **at most two children**, designated as the **left child** and the **right child**. The topmost node is called the **root**, and nodes with no children are termed **leaves** (or external nodes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A binary tree is **strictly** defined as a tree where the **degree of every node is ≤ 2**. This is the foundational definition for the entire module.

### 1.2 Key Terminology — Level, Height, and Depth

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Level of a node** | The number of edges on the path from the **root** to that node. The root is at **level 0**. |
| **Height of a node** | The number of edges on the **longest downward path** from that node to a leaf. A leaf has height **0**. |
| **Height of a tree** | The height of the **root node** (equivalently, the maximum depth of any node). |
| **Depth of a node** | Synonymous with the level of a node — distance from the root. |
| **Degree of a node** | Number of children the node possesses (0, 1, or 2 in a binary tree). |

### 1.3 Complete Binary Tree (CBT) — Formal Definition

A **Complete Binary Tree** is a binary tree in which:
- All levels, **except possibly the last**, are completely filled.
- All nodes in the **last level** are as far **left as possible** (no gaps between nodes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Full Binary Tree:** A *full* (or *proper*) binary tree requires every node to have **either 0 or 2 children**. A *complete* binary tree only requires the last level to be filled left-to-right. These are **not** the same — this is a frequent KTU exam pitfall.

### 1.4 Tree Traversals

A **traversal** is the process of visiting every node in a tree exactly once in a specific order.

| Traversal | Visit Order | Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| **Inorder** | Left → Root → Right | Symmetric / LDR |
| **Preorder** | Root → Left → Right | DLR |
| **Postorder** | Left → Right → Root | LRD |
| **Level Order** | Level by level, left to right | Breadth-First Traversal |

### 1.5 Binary Search Tree (BST) — Formal Definition

A **Binary Search Tree** is a binary tree that satisfies the **BST Property**:
- For every node `N`, all keys in the **left subtree** of `N` are **strictly less** than the key of `N`.
- For every node `N`, all keys in the **right subtree** of `N` are **strictly greater** than the key of `N`.
- Both the left and right subtrees must themselves be **BSTs**.

### 1.6 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a binary tree like a **family genealogy chart**:
> - The **root** is the eldest ancestor (great-grandparent).
> - Each person can have at most **two direct descendants** in our simplified chart (left and right child).
> - **Level** = how many generations down from the ancestor.
> - **Height** = the depth of the deepest descendant lineage.
> - A **BST** is like a *phone directory booth* — names starting with 'A-M' are on the left, 'N-Z' on the right, and at every booth, this rule repeats. This is why searching in a BST is so fast (like a binary search on a sorted array).

### 1.7 Why Trees? — Engineering Relevance

- **File Systems:** Directories and subdirectories form a tree (e.g., Linux `/home/user/documents/`).
- **Database Indexing:** B-Trees (generalization of BST) accelerate queries.
- **Compiler Design:** Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) represent parsed code.
- **Network Routing:** Decision trees in routing algorithms.
- **AI/ML:** Decision trees are foundational to machine learning classifiers.
- **Expression Evaluation:** Arithmetic expressions parsed into expression trees.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Properties of Binary Trees

Let **L** = level of a node, **H** = height of the tree, and **N** = total number of nodes.

#### Property 1: Maximum number of nodes at a given level `L`
At level 0 → 1 node (the root)
At level 1 → 2 nodes
At level 2 → 4 nodes
... and so on.

**Geometric Series Interpretation:** Each level doubles the number of nodes because every node can have at most 2 children.

#### Property 2: Maximum number of nodes in a tree of height `H`

Summing the geometric series from level 0 to level H:

$$1 + 2 + 4 + \ldots + 2^H = \sum_{i=0}^{H} 2^i$$

#### Property 3: Minimum number of nodes in a tree of height `H`
Each level must have **at least one node** (otherwise the tree wouldn't have that height). So minimum nodes = H + 1 (a **skewed tree**).

#### Property 4: Height bounds for a tree with N nodes
- **Minimum height** (best case — perfectly balanced):
$$H_{min} = \lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor$$
- **Maximum height** (worst case — completely skewed/degenerate):
$$H_{max} = N - 1$$

#### Property 5: Edge count relationship
A tree with **N** nodes has exactly **N − 1** edges (fundamental property of any tree, not just binary).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The formulas below are **frequently asked** in Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize them rigorously.

| Property / Metric | Formula (0-indexed array) | Formula (1-indexed array) | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max nodes at level $\ell$ | $2^{\ell}$ | $2^{\ell}$ | $\ell \geq 0$ |
| Max nodes in tree of height $H$ | $2^{H+1} - 1$ | $2^{H+1} - 1$ | $H \geq 0$ |
| Min nodes in tree of height $H$ | $H + 1$ | $H + 1$ | Skewed tree |
| Min height with $N$ nodes | $\lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor$ | $\lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor$ | Complete BT |
| Max height with $N$ nodes | $N - 1$ | $N - 1$ | Skewed tree |
| Left child of node at index $i$ | $2i + 1$ | $2i$ | $i \geq 0$ |
| Right child of node at index $i$ | $2i + 2$ | $2i + 1$ | $i \geq 0$ |
| Parent of node at index $i$ | $\lfloor (i - 1) / 2 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$ | $i > 0$ |
| Total nodes in array for CBT | $N$ (compact, no gaps) | $N$ | $N \geq 1$ |
| BST Search complexity (avg) | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Balanced tree |
| BST Search complexity (worst) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Skewed tree |
| Number of edges in tree with $N$ nodes | $N - 1$ | $N - 1$ | Always |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Note:** Most textbooks (and KTU) prefer **1-based indexing** for tree array representation because the parent formula becomes cleaner: $\lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$. However, C/C++ arrays are 0-indexed, so we use the adjusted formulas.

### 2.3 BST Search — Why It's Efficient

BST search exploits the **BST property** to eliminate half the search space at each step (similar to binary search on a sorted array). The recurrence relation is:

$$T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) = O(\log n) \text{ (balanced case)}$$

But for a **skewed** BST (e.g., inserting 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 in order), it degenerates to a linked list:

$$T(n) = T(n - 1) + O(1) = O(n)$$

This is why **self-balancing BSTs** (AVL, Red-Black Trees) are used in practice.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Formulas

- **Memory Allocation:** Array representation of CBT is used in **Binary Heaps** (Priority Queues in OS schedulers, Dijkstra's algorithm).
- **Huffman Coding Trees:** Used in data compression (ZIP, JPEG).
- **Database B-Trees:** Extension of BST for disk-based indexing.
- **Network Multicast:** Tree structures for efficient group communication.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Nodes at Level `L`

**Setup:** Consider a perfect binary tree where every level is completely filled.

**Step 1:** At level 0, there is exactly 1 node (the root).
$$N_0 = 1 = 2^0$$

**Step 2:** Every node at level `L−1` can have at most 2 children. Therefore, the number of nodes at level `L` is:
$$N_L = 2 \cdot N_{L-1}$$

**Step 3:** This is a geometric progression with first term $a = 1$ and common ratio $r = 2$:
$$N_L = 2^L$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum nodes at level $L = 2^L$. ∎

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Nodes in Tree of Height `H`

**Step 1:** Sum the geometric series from level 0 to level H:
$$N_{max} = \sum_{L=0}^{H} 2^L$$

**Step 2:** Apply the geometric series formula $S = a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1)$:
$$N_{max} = \frac{1 \cdot (2^{H+1} - 1)}{2 - 1} = 2^{H+1} - 1$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum nodes in a tree of height $H = 2^{H+1} - 1$. ∎

### 3.3 Derivation: Array Indexing Formulas (1-based)

**Step 1:** For node at index $i$ in the array, its left child is connected via the binary heap-like layout. In a complete binary tree, levels are filled left-to-right, so position 2 holds the left child of root, position 3 holds the right child, etc.

**Step 2:** For a node at index $i$, the left child occupies position $2i$ (skipping the right child slot of every previous parent).

**Step 3:** The right child occupies position $2i + 1$ (immediately after the left child).

**Step 4:** The parent of a node at index $i$ (for $i > 1$) is at position $\lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$ (integer division).

**Verification:** Consider node at index 5. Its parent is $\lfloor 5/2 \rfloor = 2$. Its left child is $2 \cdot 5 = 10$, right child is $2 \cdot 5 + 1 = 11$. ✓

### 3.4 Detailed Walkthrough: Tree Traversals

**Reference Tree:** Let us use the following binary tree for all traversal demonstrations:

```
              A
            /   \
           B     C
          / \     \
         D   E     F
```

#### 3.4.1 Recursive Inorder Traversal (LDR)

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**
```
INORDER(node):
    if node == NULL: return
    INORDER(node.left)
    visit(node)
    INORDER(node.right)
```

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

| Call Stack Depth | Current Node | Action | Output Buffer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | Recurse left to B | [] |
| 2 | B | Recurse left to D | [] |
| 3 | D | Recurse left (NULL), return | [] |
| 3 | D | Visit D | [D] |
| 3 | D | Recurse right (NULL), return | [D] |
| 2 | B | Visit B | [D, B] |
| 2 | B | Recurse right to E | [D, B] |
| 3 | E | Recurse left (NULL), return | [D, B] |
| 3 | E | Visit E | [D, B, E] |
| 3 | E | Recurse right (NULL), return | [D, B, E] |
| 1 | A | Visit A | [D, B, E, A] |
| 1 | A | Recurse right to C | [D, B, E, A] |
| 2 | C | Recurse left (NULL), return | [D, B, E, A] |
| 2 | C | Visit C | [D, B, E, A, C] |
| 2 | C | Recurse right to F | [D, B, E, A, C] |
| 3 | F | Visit F | [D, B, E, A, C, F] |

**Final Inorder Sequence:** `D → B → E → A → C → F`

#### 3.4.2 Recursive Preorder Traversal (DLR)

**Algorithm:**
```
PREORDER(node):
    if node == NULL: return
    visit(node)
    PREORDER(node.left)
    PREORDER(node.right)
```

**Final Preorder Sequence:** `A → B → D → E → C → F`

#### 3.4.3 Recursive Postorder Traversal (LRD)

**Algorithm:**
```
POSTORDER(node):
    if node == NULL: return
    POSTORDER(node.left)
    POSTORDER(node.right)
    visit(node)
```

**Final Postorder Sequence:** `D → E → B → F → C → A`

#### 3.4.4 Non-Recursive Inorder Traversal (Using Stack)

**Algorithm:**
```
INORDER_ITER(root):
    stack = empty
    current = root
    while current != NULL OR stack is not empty:
        while current != NULL:
            PUSH(current)
            current = current.left
        current = POP(stack)
        visit(current)
        current = current.right
```

**Execution Trace:**

| Step | Action | Stack (top→bottom) | Visited Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Push A, go left to B | [A] | [] |
| 2 | Push B, go left to D | [A, B] | [] |
| 3 | Push D, go left (NULL) | [A, B, D] | [] |
| 4 | Pop D, visit, go right | [A, B] | [D] |
| 5 | Pop B, visit, go right to E | [A] | [D, B] |
| 6 | Push E, go left (NULL) | [A, E] | [D, B] |
| 7 | Pop E, visit, go right | [A] | [D, B, E] |
| 8 | Pop A, visit, go right to C | [] | [D, B, E, A] |
| 9 | Push C, go left (NULL) | [C] | [D, B, E, A] |
| 10 | Pop C, visit, go right to F | [] | [D, B, E, A, C] |
| 11 | Push F, go left (NULL) | [F] | [D, B, E, A, C] |
| 12 | Pop F, visit, go right | [] | [D, B, E, A, C, F] |

**Final Non-Recursive Inorder:** `D → B → E → A → C → F` ✓ (Matches recursive)

#### 3.4.5 Non-Recursive Preorder Traversal

**Algorithm:**
```
PREORDER_ITER(root):
    if root == NULL: return
    stack = [root]
    while stack is not empty:
        node = POP(stack)
        visit(node)
        if node.right != NULL: PUSH(node.right)
        if node.left != NULL: PUSH(node.left)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why push right first, then left?** Stack is LIFO. By pushing the right child first, the left child is popped (and visited) first, preserving the **Root → Left → Right** order.

### 3.5 Binary Search Tree Operations — Detailed Walkthrough

**Sequence of Insertions:** `50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80`

#### Step 3.5.1: Insertion Algorithm
```
INSERT(root, key):
    if root == NULL: return newNode(key)
    if key < root.key: root.left = INSERT(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.key: root.right = INSERT(root.right, key)
    return root
```

**Step-by-step insertion trace:**

| Step | Key Inserted | Comparison Path | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | — | Create root |
| 2 | 30 | 30 < 50 | Go left; create node |
| 3 | 70 | 70 > 50 | Go right; create node |
| 4 | 20 | 20 < 50 → 20 < 30 | Go left twice; create node |
| 5 | 40 | 40 < 50 → 40 > 30 | Go left, then right; create node |
| 6 | 60 | 60 > 50 → 60 < 70 | Go right, then left; create node |
| 7 | 80 | 80 > 50 → 80 > 70 | Go right twice; create node |

**Resulting BST:**
```
            50
          /    \
        30      70
       /  \    /  \
      20  40  60  80
```

**Inorder Traversal of this BST:** `20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80` (sorted!) — this is a **fundamental property** of BSTs.

#### Step 3.5.2: Search Operation
**Search for key 40:**
1. Start at root (50). 40 < 50 → go left.
2. At node 30. 40 > 30 → go right.
3. At node 40. Match found! Return TRUE.

**Time Complexity:** $O(h)$ where $h$ is the height of the tree.

#### Step 3.5.3: Deletion Operation — The Three Critical Cases

**Algorithm:**
```
DELETE(root, key):
    if root == NULL: return root
    if key < root.key: root.left = DELETE(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.key: root.right = DELETE(root.right, key)
    else:
        # Node found - apply cases:
        Case 1: root.left == NULL: return root.right
        Case 2: root.right == NULL: return root.left
        Case 3: Both children exist:
            successor = MIN(root.right)        # Inorder successor
            root.key = successor.key
            root.right = DELETE(root.right, successor.key)
    return root
```

**Delete key = 20 (Case 1: Leaf Node)**

```
Before:                  After:
        50                    50
       /  \                  /  \
      30   70               30   70
     /  \  / \               \  / \
    20  40 60 80             40 60 80
```

**Action:** Node 20 has no children → simply remove it.

**Delete key = 30 (Case 2: One Child)**

```
Before:                  After:
        50                    50
       /  \                  /  \
      30   70               40   70
     /  \  / \                \  / \
    20  40 60 80              -- 60 80
```

**Action:** Node 30 has only right child (40) → replace 30 with 40.

**Delete key = 50 (Case 3: Two Children) — Most Complex**

**Sub-steps:**
1. Find the **inorder successor** of 50: the smallest node in the right subtree.
2. Starting at 70, go left → 60. 60 has no left child → **60 is the successor**.
3. **Copy** 60's value into 50's position.
4. **Delete** 60 from the right subtree (60 is a leaf, so Case 1 applies).

```
Before:                       After:
        50                          60
       /  \                        /  \
      30   70                     30   70
     /  \  / \                   /  \    \
    20  40 60 80                20  40   80
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** In Case 3, students often delete the successor node from the **wrong subtree** or forget to maintain the BST property after replacement. Always perform replacement **first**, then recursively delete the successor's original position.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: BST vs. Other Data Structures

| Operation | Sorted Array | Linked List | Balanced BST (AVL) | Unbalanced BST |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Search | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ worst |
| Insertion | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ at head | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ worst |
| Deletion | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ at node | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ worst |
| Space | $O(n)$ contiguous | $O(n)$ scattered | $O(n)$ scattered | $O(n)$ scattered |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reference Binary Tree (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[A: 50] --> B[B: 30]
    A --> C[C: 70]
    B --> D[D: 20]
    B --> E[E: 40]
    C --> F[F: 60]
    C --> G[G: 80]
```

### 4.2 Complete Binary Tree Stored in Array (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Array1["Array Storage (1-indexed)"]
        A1["Index 1: A"]
        A2["Index 2: B"]
        A3["Index 3: C"]
        A4["Index 4: D"]
        A5["Index 5: E"]
        A6["Index 6: F"]
        A7["Index 7: G"]
    end
    subgraph TreeStruct["Tree Representation"]
        T1[A: 50] --> T2[B: 30]
        T1 --> T3[C: 70]
        T2 --> T4[D: 20]
        T2 --> T5[E: 40]
        T3 --> T6[F: 60]
        T3 --> T7[G: 80]
    end
```

**Array Mapping Visualization:**

| Index | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | 50 | 30 | 70 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 |
| Role | Root | Left of 1 | Right of 1 | Left of 2 | Right of 2 | Left of 3 | Right of 3 |

**Index Formula Verification:**
- Left child of index 1: $2 \cdot 1 = 2$ ✓
- Right child of index 1: $2 \cdot 1 + 1 = 3$ ✓
- Parent of index 5: $\lfloor 5 / 2 \rfloor = 2$ ✓
- Left child of index 3: $2 \cdot 3 = 6$ ✓

### 4.3 BST Deletion — Case 3 Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start DELETE root, key] --> CheckNull{root is NULL?}
    CheckNull -->|Yes| ReturnNull[Return NULL]
    CheckNull -->|No| CompareKey{key vs root.key}
    CompareKey -->|key < root.key| GoLeft[root.left = DELETE left, key]
    CompareKey -->|key > root.key| GoRight[root.right = DELETE right, key]
    CompareKey -->|key == root.key| FoundNode[Node Found]
    FoundNode --> CheckLeft{root.left is NULL?}
    CheckLeft -->|Yes| Case1[Return root.right - Case 1]
    CheckLeft -->|No| CheckRight{root.right is NULL?}
    CheckRight -->|Yes| Case2[Return root.left - Case 2]
    CheckRight -->|No| Case3[Find inorder successor in right subtree]
    Case3 --> Copy[Copy successor.key to root.key]
    Copy --> Recur[Recursively delete successor from right subtree]
```

### 4.4 Non-Recursive Inorder Traversal — Stack Operation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Init[Initialize: stack=empty, current=root] --> LoopStart{current != NULL OR stack not empty?}
    LoopStart -->|No| Done[End Traversal]
    LoopStart -->|Yes| InnerWhile{current != NULL?}
    InnerWhile -->|Yes| Push[Push current onto stack, then current = current.left]
    InnerWhile -->|No| PopNode[Pop node from stack]
    Push --> InnerWhile
    PopNode --> Visit[Visit popped node]
    Visit --> MoveRight[current = current.right]
    MoveRight --> LoopStart
```

### 4.5 Inorder Successor Identification Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Node to Delete: X has two children] --> R1[Go to right subtree of X]
    R1 --> R2[Then go as far left as possible]
    R2 --> R3[That leftmost node is the Inorder Successor]
    R3 --> R4[Copy successor's value to X]
    R4 --> R5[Delete the original successor node]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic:** The inorder successor is the node that would come **immediately after** the target in the **sorted inorder sequence**. For BST above, deleting 50: sorted sequence is 20, 30, 40, **50, 60**, 70, 80 → successor of 50 is 60.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with respect to binary trees:**
**(a)** Level of a node
**(b)** Height of a tree
**(c)** Complete binary tree

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Level of a node:** The level of a node is the number of edges on the unique path from the **root** to that node. The root node is at level 0. Its immediate children are at level 1, and so on. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Height of a tree:** The height of a binary tree is defined as the number of edges on the **longest path** from the root node to any leaf node. Equivalently, it is the **maximum level** of any node in the tree. A tree with a single node has height 0. *(1 mark)*

**(c) Complete Binary Tree:** A binary tree in which all levels, **except possibly the last**, are completely filled, and all nodes in the last level appear as **far left as possible**. *(1 mark)*

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Binary Search Tree (BST)? State the BST property. Mention any two applications of BST.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** is a binary tree data structure where each node contains a key and satisfies the **BST property**:

- For any node `N`, all keys in the **left subtree** of `N` are **less than** the key of `N$.
- For any node `N`, all keys in the **right subtree** of `N` are **greater than** the key of `N`.
- Both left and right subtrees are themselves BSTs. *(2 marks)*

**Two Applications of BST:**
1. **Efficient searching** in databases and search engines (average $O(\log n)$ lookup time).
2. **Sorting** — inorder traversal of a BST produces a sorted sequence, enabling BST sort. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **array representation of a complete binary tree**. Derive the formulas for finding the **left child**, **right child**, and **parent** of a node stored at index `i` (1-based indexing). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **recursive and non-recursive algorithms** for **inorder traversal** of a binary tree. Show the working of both on a sample tree with at least 7 nodes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Array Representation of Complete Binary Tree:**

In the array representation, nodes of a complete binary tree are stored **level by level, from left to right**, in a one-dimensional array. Consider the following complete binary tree:

```
              A (index 1)
            /   \
           B     C  (indices 2, 3)
          / \   / \
         D  E  F   G (indices 4, 5, 6, 7)
```

| Index | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |

**Derivation of Indexing Formulas (1-based):**

- **Left child of node at index `i`:** $LC = 2i$

  *Reasoning:* For a node at index 1 (root), left child should be at index 2. For node at index 2, left child at index 4. Pattern: position $2i$.

- **Right child of node at index `i`:** $RC = 2i + 1$

  *Reasoning:* Right child always immediately follows the left child → $2i + 1$.

- **Parent of node at index `i` (where $i > 1$):** $P = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$

  *Reasoning:* For $i = 2$ or $i = 3$, parent is index 1. For $i = 4$ or $i = 5$, parent is index 2. Pattern: $\lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$. *(7 marks)*

**(b) Recursive and Non-Recursive Inorder Traversal:**

**Recursive Algorithm:**
```
void inorderRec(Node* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return;
    inorderRec(root->left);
    printf("%d ", root->data);
    inorderRec(root->right);
}
```

**Non-Recursive Algorithm (Using Stack):**
```
void inorderIter(Node* root) {
    Stack s;
    Node* current = root;
    while (current != NULL || !s.isEmpty()) {
        while (current != NULL) {
            s.push(current);
            current = current->left;
        }
        current = s.pop();
        printf("%d ", current->data);
        current = current->right;
    }
}
```

**Working on Sample Tree:**

```
            1
          /   \
         2     3
        / \   / \
       4  5  6   7
```

**Recursive Trace:**

| Call | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| inorder(1) → inorder(2) → inorder(4) | Visit 4 (no children) | 4 |
| Back to inorder(2) | Visit 2 | 4 2 |
| inorder(5) | Visit 5 | 4 2 5 |
| Back to inorder(1) | Visit 1 | 4 2 5 1 |
| inorder(3) → inorder(6) | Visit 6 | 4 2 5 1 6 |
| inorder(7) | Visit 7 | 4 2 5 1 6 7 |

**Final Inorder Output:** `4 2 5 1 6 3 7` *(7 marks)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define a **Binary Search Tree**. Explain the **algorithm for insertion** in a BST with a suitable example. Show the resulting BST when the following keys are inserted in the given order: `45, 25, 65, 15, 35, 55, 75, 10`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three cases of deletion** in a Binary Search Tree. Demonstrate each case by deleting one node from the BST constructed in part (a). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BST Insertion:**

**Definition:** A Binary Search Tree is a binary tree where for every node `N`: keys in the left subtree < key(N) < keys in the right subtree.

**Insertion Algorithm:**
```
Node* insert(Node* root, int key) {
    if (root == NULL) return createNode(key);
    if (key < root->data) root->left = insert(root->left, key);
    else if (key > root->data) root->right = insert(root->right, key);
    return root;
}
```

**Construction trace for sequence 45, 25, 65, 15, 35, 55, 75, 10:**

| Step | Key | Comparison | Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 45 | Tree empty | Root |
| 2 | 25 | 25 < 45 | Left of 45 |
| 3 | 65 | 65 > 45 | Right of 45 |
| 4 | 15 | 15 < 45 → 15 < 25 | Left of 25 |
| 5 | 35 | 35 < 45 → 35 > 25 | Right of 25 |
| 6 | 55 | 55 > 45 → 55 < 65 | Left of 65 |
| 7 | 75 | 75 > 45 → 75 > 65 | Right of 65 |
| 8 | 10 | 10 < 45 → 10 < 25 → 10 < 15 | Left of 15 |

**Resulting BST:**
```
              45
            /    \
          25      65
         /  \    /  \
        15  35  55  75
        /
       10
```
*(7 marks)*

**(b) BST Deletion — Three Cases:**

**Algorithm with Three Cases:**
```
Node* delete(Node* root, int key) {
    if (root == NULL) return root;
    if (key < root->data) root->left = delete(root->left, key);
    else if (key > root->data) root->right = delete(root->right, key);
    else {
        if (root->left == NULL) return root->right;     // Case 1 & 2
        else if (root->right == NULL) return root->left;
        // Case 3: Two children
        Node* succ = findMin(root->right);
        root->data = succ->data;
        root->right = delete(root->right, succ->data);
    }
    return root;
}
```

**Demonstration:**

**Case 1 — Delete leaf node (10):** Node 10 is a leaf (no children). Simply remove it.

```
Before:                    After:
        45                     45
       /  \                   /  \
      25   65                25   65
     / \   / \               / \   / \
    15 35 55 75             15 35 55 75
    /                        --
   10
```

**Case 2 — Delete node with one child (15):** Node 15 has only one child (originally 10, but after deleting 10 above, it has no children — let us use a fresh example. Delete 55 which has no children here, but for Case 2 demonstration, consider deleting a node with one child: delete node 65 which has both children here. So instead, let us use a clearer example).
